past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 20 years ago, poorly controlled
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 12 years ago
Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, mild, former smoker
family_history: Father had a pacemaker placed at age 70 for unknown rhythm problem, died at 74 of heart failure
Mother had hypertension and stroke at age 68, died at 72
One brother with type 2 diabetes and hypertension
No known family history of sudden cardiac death or seizure disorders

social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, 20-pack-year history, quit 8 years ago
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a two-story home, bedroom upstairs
social_history.exercise: Used to walk 2 miles daily, stopped 3 months ago due to fatigue and intermittent dizziness

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech fluent, normal rate and prosody, no dysarthria or aphasia. Attention intact, able to perform serial 7s. Short-term recall 3/3 at 5 minutes. No confusion, no post-ictal features noted at time of evaluation, approximately 45 minutes after the event.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3mm to 2mm. No papilledema on fundoscopy. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact, no nystagmus, no diplopia. CN V: Sensation intact V1-V3 bilaterally, masseter strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation, no facial droop. CN VIII: Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, uvula midline. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue midline without fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal. Tone normal in all extremities, no rigidity, no spasticity. Strength 5/5 throughout all tested muscle groups bilaterally including deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, finger abductors, hip flexors, knee extensors, ankle dorsiflexors, and plantarflexors. No pronator drift. No tremor.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch intact throughout. Pinprick intact. Proprioception intact at bilateral great toes. Vibration mildly diminished at bilateral great toes but intact at ankles, consistent with mild length-dependent sensory neuropathy.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria or intention tremor. Heel-to-shin testing intact bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait, normal base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue laceration. No evidence of urinary incontinence. Small abrasion on left elbow from fall. Witness (wife) describes approximately 5-10 seconds of bilateral upper extremity jerking movements during the episode, which initially raised concern for seizure.

patient.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of sudden loss of consciousness with brief shaking movements
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Megan Fletcher is a 55-year-old man with a long-standing history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and COPD who presents to the emergency department after her third episode of sudden loss of consciousness in the past six weeks. Today's episode occurred while she was standing in the kitchen preparing lunch at approximately 11:30 AM. Her wife, who was in the adjacent room, heard a loud thud and rushed in to find him lying on the floor. She reports that she was unresponsive and her arms were shaking for approximately 5 to 10 seconds. She describes the movements as irregular jerking of both arms, without sustained tonic stiffening or rhythmic clonic activity. Her eyes were open and she appeared pale. She regained consciousness within approximately 15 to 20 seconds and was immediately coherent, asking her wife what happened. There was no tongue biting, no frothing at the mouth, and no urinary or fecal incontinence. She had no post-ictal confusion, drowsiness, or headache. She reports no preceding aura, no unusual smells or tastes, and no warning of the impending event. Her first episode occurred approximately six weeks ago while watching television. She briefly 'blacked out' for a few seconds without any witnessed convulsive movements. The second episode occurred three weeks ago while climbing the stairs at home; she felt sudden lightheadedness and then lost consciousness, falling backward onto the landing. Her wife witnessed brief arm jerking during that episode as well. She was evaluated by her primary care physician after the second episode, who referred him to neurology for suspected new-onset seizures and started him on levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily three days ago, which she has been taking. She reports that over the past two to three months, she has felt increasingly fatigued and has noticed episodes of dizziness, particularly with exertion such as climbing stairs or walking briskly. She occasionally feels her heart 'skipping' but denies sustained palpitations, chest pain, or dyspnea at rest. She has not had any febrile illness, recent medication changes other than the levetiracetam, or head trauma prior to these events. She denies any history of seizures, and there is no family history of epilepsy. Her wife emphasizes that she becomes immediately alert after each episode, which she finds unusual compared to her understanding of seizures.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-modulated, reactive to eye opening
background.anterior_rhythms: Low-to-medium amplitude beta activity, symmetric, no focal slowing
background.sleep_features: Brief periods of drowsiness with symmetric vertex waves and sleep spindles. No epileptiform discharges in drowsiness or sleep.

artifacts.description: Intermittent muscle artifact, does not obscure underlying cerebral activity
Prominent ECG artifact noted, reflecting cardiac rhythm irregularity; does not interfere with EEG interpretation

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. Mild diffuse symmetrical slowing during hyperventilation, which is a normal physiological response. No epileptiform discharges provoked. No focal abnormalities.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at frequencies 1-30 Hz. Normal photic driving response at 12-18 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and drowsy EEG. No epileptiform abnormalities. No seizures captured during this 45-minute routine recording. Prominent ECG artifact was noted, which may warrant separate cardiac evaluation. This normal EEG does not exclude a diagnosis of epilepsy but provides no supportive evidence for it. Clinical correlation is essential.
eeg.limitations: Routine 45-minute recording. A single normal interictal EEG has limited sensitivity (approximately 50%) for detecting epilepsy. Prolonged or ambulatory EEG monitoring may be considered if epilepsy remains a clinical concern.
recommended_actions: If clinical suspicion for seizure persists, consider prolonged ambulatory EEG or inpatient video-EEG monitoring
Given prominent ECG artifact suggesting cardiac rhythm abnormality, recommend formal cardiac rhythm evaluation

ecg.rhythm: Sinus rhythm with second-degree AV block, Mobitz type II
findings: Sinus P waves at atrial rate of 78 bpm with intermittent failure of conduction (2:1 and 3:1 conduction ratios observed)
Constant PR interval in conducted beats, consistent with Mobitz type II second-degree AV block
Right bundle branch block pattern (rsR' in V1, wide S in I and V6)
Left anterior fascicular block (left axis deviation beyond -45 degrees, qR pattern in aVL)
Bifascicular block (RBBB + LAFB) with superimposed intermittent Mobitz type II AV block
Borderline QTc prolongation at 486 ms
No acute ST-segment elevation or depression
ecg.interpretation: 12-lead ECG demonstrates sinus rhythm with second-degree AV block, Mobitz type II, with variable 2:1 and 3:1 conduction, resulting in a ventricular rate of approximately 52 bpm. There is bifascicular block consisting of right bundle branch block and left anterior fascicular block. The combination of bifascicular block with Mobitz type II AV block indicates advanced infranodal conduction disease with high risk of progression to complete heart block. This finding is clinically significant and likely explains the patient's recurrent syncopal episodes.
ecg.clinical_correlation: The intermittent Mobitz type II second-degree AV block with bifascicular block is a high-risk ECG finding that correlates with the patient's episodic syncope. The intermittent nature of the block explains why the patient has discrete episodes of loss of consciousness rather than persistent symptoms. When conduction fails transiently (particularly during higher-ratio block such as 3:1), the resulting pause in ventricular activity causes abrupt cerebral hypoperfusion, syncope, and reflex convulsive movements (convulsive syncope). This is a Class I indication for permanent pacemaker implantation.

BMP.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, likely prerenal from mild dehydration or related to hydrochlorothiazide use
Elevated, consistent with known diabetes mellitus, non-fasting

Cardiac_markers.clinical_significance: Normal, no evidence of acute myocardial injury
Mildly elevated, may reflect subclinical cardiac dysfunction related to intermittent bradycardia and longstanding hypertension

Antiepileptic_drug_level.clinical_significance: Subtherapeutic, patient was started on levetiracetam only 3 days ago and has not reached steady state; however, clinical picture does not support epilepsy diagnosis

labs.interpretation: Laboratory studies reveal no acute metabolic cause for syncope. Electrolytes are within normal limits, effectively ruling out hypokalemia, hyperkalemia, hyponatremia, or hypocalcemia as contributors to cardiac conduction abnormalities. Troponin is normal, excluding acute myocardial injury. BNP is mildly elevated, suggesting subclinical cardiac dysfunction. BUN is mildly elevated, likely related to hydrochlorothiazide. Glucose and HbA1c are elevated in the context of known diabetes. TSH is normal, ruling out hypothyroidism as a cause of bradycardia. Levetiracetam level is subtherapeutic as it was recently started, though the clinical presentation does not support an epilepsy diagnosis.
abnormal_values_summary: BUN: 22 mg/dL - Mildly elevated, likely prerenal from mild dehydration or related to hydrochlorothiazide use
Glucose: 161 mg/dL - Elevated, consistent with known diabetes mellitus, non-fasting
BNP: 204 pg/mL - Mildly elevated, may reflect subclinical cardiac dysfunction related to intermittent bradycardia and longstanding hypertension
HbA1c: 7.8 % - Indicates suboptimally controlled diabetes mellitus
Levetiracetam level: 8.9 mcg/mL - Subtherapeutic, patient was started on levetiracetam only 3 days ago and has not reached steady state; however, clinical picture does not support epilepsy diagnosis

Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Mildly reduced, likely related to longstanding hypertension and intermittent bradycardia
Moderate concentric left ventricular hypertrophy consistent with longstanding hypertension
Mildly dilated, likely from chronic hypertension and diastolic dysfunction

Echocardiogram.value: Mild sclerosis, no stenosis, no regurgitation
Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Degenerative changes, may contribute to conduction system calcification

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrates moderate concentric left ventricular hypertrophy consistent with longstanding hypertension. Left ventricular systolic function is mildly reduced with an estimated ejection fraction of 50%. Grade I diastolic dysfunction (impaired relaxation pattern) is present. Left atrium is mildly dilated. Aortic valve demonstrates mild sclerosis without hemodynamically significant stenosis. No pericardial effusion, intracardiac masses, or thrombus. The structural findings are consistent with hypertensive heart disease. Aortic valve sclerosis and interventricular septal hypertrophy raise the possibility of degenerative conduction system disease contributing to the patient's AV block.
abnormal_values_summary: Left ventricular ejection fraction: 50% % - Mildly reduced, likely related to longstanding hypertension and intermittent bradycardia
Left ventricular wall thickness (septum): 1.3 cm - Moderate concentric left ventricular hypertrophy consistent with longstanding hypertension
Left ventricular wall thickness (posterior): 1.2 cm - Concentric LVH
Left atrial diameter: 4.1 cm - Mildly dilated, likely from chronic hypertension and diastolic dysfunction
Aortic valve: Mild sclerosis, no stenosis, no regurgitation qualitative - Degenerative changes, may contribute to conduction system calcification
Diastolic function: Grade I diastolic dysfunction (impaired relaxation) qualitative - Consistent with hypertensive heart disease

output.rhythm: Sinus rhythm with intermittent second-degree AV block, Mobitz type II, with episodes of high-grade AV block
findings: Underlying sinus rhythm with atrial rate averaging 74 bpm
Average ventricular rate 54 bpm (range 32-72 bpm)
Frequent Mobitz type II second-degree AV block with variable conduction ratios (2:1, 3:1, and 4:1)
Three episodes of high-grade AV block with pauses of 3.8, 4.1, and 5.2 seconds
Longest pause of 5.2 seconds occurred at 14:22 while patient was standing, associated with patient-activated event marker and notation of 'felt dizzy'
Persistent bifascicular block (RBBB + LAFB) throughout recording
Rare isolated PVCs (18 total in 24 hours)
No sustained or non-sustained ventricular tachycardia
No atrial fibrillation or other supraventricular arrhythmias
output.interpretation: 24-hour Holter monitor confirms intermittent second-degree AV block, Mobitz type II, with episodes of high-grade AV block and pauses up to 5.2 seconds. The longest pause was symptomatic and correlated with the patient's event diary. Persistent bifascicular block throughout. The intermittent nature of the high-grade block explains the episodic nature of the patient's syncopal events. This represents advanced infranodal conduction disease with a high risk of progression to complete heart block and is a Class I indication for permanent pacemaker implantation.
output.clinical_correlation: The documented pauses up to 5.2 seconds during Holter monitoring, with symptom correlation, definitively establish the diagnosis of cardiac syncope due to intermittent high-grade AV block. The convulsive movements witnessed during syncopal episodes represent convulsive syncope from cerebral hypoperfusion, not epileptic seizures. Levetiracetam should be discontinued. Urgent permanent pacemaker implantation is indicated.

Tilt_table_test.clinical_significance: Bradycardia reflecting underlying AV conduction disease
Mild drop but within normal limits for orthostatic response
Blunted chronotropic response, limited by conduction disease
Withheld due to underlying conduction disease and risk of further AV block
No cardioinhibitory or vasodepressor response. Vasovagal syncope is excluded as the mechanism.

output.interpretation: Tilt table test is negative for vasovagal syncope. No cardioinhibitory or vasodepressor response was provoked during 45 minutes of head-up tilt. The mildly blunted chronotropic response to tilt is consistent with the patient's underlying conduction disease but is insufficient to cause syncope during this controlled test. The absence of a vasovagal mechanism directs attention to the intermittent cardiac conduction disease identified on ECG and Holter monitoring as the cause of the patient's syncopal episodes.
abnormal_values_summary: Baseline supine BP: 146/86 mmHg - Elevated baseline reflecting chronic hypertension
Baseline supine HR: 54 bpm - Bradycardia reflecting underlying AV conduction disease
70-degree tilt HR at 3 min: 58 bpm - Blunted chronotropic response, limited by conduction disease

output.rhythm: Sinus rhythm with progressive worsening of AV block during exercise
findings: Resting: 2:1 AV block, ventricular rate 38 bpm
Stage 1 Bruce protocol (1.7 mph, 10% grade): atrial rate increased to 96 bpm but persistent 2:1 block, ventricular rate 48 bpm
Stage 2 attempted but test terminated at 1 minute 30 seconds due to presyncope, blood pressure drop to 96/58, and one 3.8-second pause
Peak atrial rate 104 bpm, peak ventricular rate 52 bpm (markedly blunted due to AV block)
No exercise-induced ventricular arrhythmias
No ischemic ST changes beyond baseline conduction abnormality
output.interpretation: Exercise stress test limited by symptomatic presyncope and hemodynamic compromise. AV block worsened with exercise (paradoxical response consistent with infranodal block, as increased sinus rate exposes the block). This behavior is characteristic of Mobitz type II block below the bundle of His and confirms the infranodal location of the conduction disease. No evidence of exercise-induced ischemia, though interpretation is limited by RBBB. This result strongly supports the indication for permanent pacemaker implantation.
output.clinical_correlation: The worsening of AV block with exercise is a hallmark of infranodal conduction disease, in contrast to AV nodal block which typically improves with exercise and sympathetic stimulation. This finding reinforces the need for permanent pacing and suggests that the patient's exertional fatigue and dizziness over recent months are attributable to chronotropic incompetence from progressive conduction disease.

findings.location: Basal interventricular septum, extending to membranous septum
findings.size: Diffuse, approximately 2 cm in craniocaudal extent

signal_characteristics.T1: Elevated native T1 values (1120 ms) in basal septum
signal_characteristics.late_gadolinium_enhancement: Mid-wall linear pattern of late gadolinium enhancement in the basal interventricular septum at the level of the conduction system, non-ischemic pattern

signal_characteristics.T1: Mildly elevated diffusely (1080 ms), suggesting diffuse interstitial fibrosis
signal_characteristics.late_gadolinium_enhancement: No focal enhancement beyond the septal finding

additional_observations: Aortic valve calcification at the annulus
Normal right ventricular size and function, no fatty infiltration (ARVC excluded)
No evidence of cardiac sarcoidosis (no patchy mid-wall or epicardial enhancement)
No evidence of cardiac amyloidosis (no diffuse subendocardial enhancement, ECV not markedly elevated)
output.impression: Cardiac MRI demonstrates mid-wall fibrosis in the basal interventricular septum at the level of the bundle of His and proximal bundle branches. This pattern is consistent with degenerative fibrosis of the conduction system (Lenegre disease), providing a structural explanation for the patient's bifascicular block and intermittent Mobitz type II AV block. Concentric left ventricular hypertrophy with mildly elevated diffuse native T1 is consistent with hypertensive heart disease with diffuse interstitial fibrosis. LVEF is mildly reduced at 50%. No evidence of infiltrative cardiomyopathy, sarcoidosis, or ARVC.
recommended_actions: Permanent pacemaker implantation for symptomatic high-grade AV block
Consider dual-chamber (DDD) pacemaker given preserved but mildly reduced LVEF
Continued aggressive management of hypertension to prevent further LVH progression
Follow-up echocardiography in 6-12 months to monitor LVEF

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Mid-wall septal fibrosis at the level of the conduction system in patient with progressive infranodal AV block and bifascicular block

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Hypertensive heart disease with conduction system involvement
differential_by_imaging.rationale: Concentric LVH with diffuse interstitial fibrosis; longstanding hypertension may accelerate degenerative conduction system disease

findings.location: Periventricular white matter and centrum semiovale
findings.size: Scattered punctate to small confluent foci

additional_observations: No mass lesion or enhancing lesion
output.impression: Brain MRI demonstrates moderate chronic small vessel ischemic disease consistent with the patient's cardiovascular risk factors (hypertension, diabetes). No acute infarction, hemorrhage, or mass. No structural brain lesion to explain seizures or syncope. The neuroimaging findings do not support an intracranial etiology for the patient's transient loss of consciousness.
recommended_actions: Focus diagnostic workup on cardiac conduction disease

background.sleep_features: Normal NREM sleep architecture with vertex waves, sleep spindles, and K-complexes. No epileptiform discharges in sleep.

artifacts.description: Intermittent prominent ECG artifact with irregular RR intervals noted during portions of recording

output.impression: 48-hour continuous EEG monitoring is normal. No epileptiform discharges (spikes, sharp waves, spike-and-wave complexes) were identified during wakefulness or sleep. No electrographic seizures were captured. One clinical event was reported by nursing staff during the monitoring period in which the patient experienced brief lightheadedness; EEG during this event showed diffuse attenuation for 3 seconds consistent with cerebral hypoperfusion, not an epileptic discharge. Simultaneous cardiac telemetry showed a 3.4-second pause during this event. This EEG effectively rules out epilepsy and supports a cardiac mechanism for the patient's events.
output.limitations: No habitual clinical events (syncope with convulsive movements) occurred during the monitoring period.
recommended_actions: Discontinue levetiracetam as there is no evidence of epilepsy
Cardiac rhythm management is the priority

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Cardiac syncope due to intermittent second-degree AV block (Mobitz type II) with bifascicular block

differential.key_distinguishing: Witnessed brief convulsive movements raised initial concern for seizure, but movements were brief (5-10 seconds), irregular, and consistent with convulsive syncope from cerebral hypoperfusion. No post-ictal confusion, no tongue bite, no incontinence. Normal EEG on routine and prolonged monitoring. Immediate return to baseline cognition after events.

No identifiable trigger such as pain, prolonged standing, or emotional stress. Episodes occurred during various activities. No prodromal symptoms of nausea, warmth, or diaphoresis. Age and cardiovascular history favor cardiac etiology. Negative tilt table test.

Patient is on multiple antihypertensives including tamsulosin and hydrochlorothiazide which can cause orthostatic hypotension. However, episodes occurred in varied positions including seated. No consistent positional trigger. Orthostatic vitals not severely abnormal.
No focal neurological deficits during or after events. TIA rarely causes isolated loss of consciousness. Normal neurological examination. Normal brain MRI except age-related small vessel disease.

No psychiatric comorbidity history. Events are brief, stereotyped, and associated with pallor (objective sign). Immediate return to consciousness without emotional distress. ECG findings provide clear alternative cardiac explanation.

optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mobitz type II second-degree AV block with bifascicular block (RBBB + LAFB)
Intermittent high-grade AV block with pauses

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive labs including CBC, BMP, troponin, BNP, TSH, and magnesium
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No metabolic cause for syncope or conduction disease, normal troponin, mildly elevated BNP

optimal_actions.action: Recognize convulsive movements as convulsive syncope rather than epileptic seizure
Obtain EEG to formally evaluate for epileptiform activity
Urgent cardiology consultation for pacemaker evaluation
Obtain transthoracic echocardiogram to evaluate for structural heart disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: LVH consistent with hypertension, mildly reduced LVEF

optimal_actions.action: Discontinue levetiracetam as epilepsy diagnosis is not supported
Admit to telemetry unit for monitoring pending pacemaker implantation

critical_actions: Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately and identify Mobitz type II AV block with bifascicular block
Recognize that the witnessed convulsive movements represent convulsive syncope, not epileptic seizures
Urgent cardiology consultation for permanent pacemaker implantation
Discontinue levetiracetam, which was inappropriately started for a misdiagnosis of seizure
Have transcutaneous pacing equipment available in case of deterioration to complete heart block
contraindicated_actions: Continuing or escalating antiepileptic drug therapy for a misdiagnosed seizure disorder
Discharging the patient without cardiac monitoring and pacemaker evaluation given the high-risk conduction abnormality
Administering AV-nodal blocking agents (beta-blockers, non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers, digoxin) that could worsen AV block
Performing lumbar puncture when there is no clinical indication for it
Attributing the episodes to vasovagal syncope without performing appropriate cardiac workup
Administering IV adenosine which would worsen AV conduction
key_reasoning_points: Brief convulsive movements (myoclonic jerks) during syncope are common and represent convulsive syncope from transient cerebral hypoperfusion, not epileptic seizures; they are typically brief (<15 seconds), irregular, and not associated with post-ictal confusion

The key clinical clues favoring cardiac syncope over seizure include: no post-ictal confusion, immediate return to full alertness, no tongue biting or incontinence, pallor during the event, and episodes occurring during varied activities without a seizure-typical semiology

Mobitz type II AV block is an infranodal conduction abnormality with a high risk of progression to complete heart block, making it a Class I indication for permanent pacemaker implantation even in asymptomatic patients
Bifascicular block (RBBB + LAFB) with superimposed Mobitz type II block indicates disease in three of four fascicles of the infranodal conduction system, representing trifascicular disease
The intermittent nature of the AV block explains the episodic presentation, as syncope occurs only during transient periods of high-grade block with prolonged pauses

The normal EEG supports the cardiac rather than epileptic etiology but does not definitively rule out epilepsy; however, taken together with the clinical presentation and ECG findings, it strongly supports cardiac syncope

The patient was inappropriately started on levetiracetam based on a misdiagnosis of seizures, highlighting the importance of obtaining an ECG in all patients presenting with transient loss of consciousness
Worsening of AV block with exercise (as seen on stress testing) is characteristic of infranodal block, distinguishing it from AV nodal block which typically improves with sympathetic stimulation

clinical_pearls: Up to 12% of patients with cardiac syncope have brief myoclonic jerks (convulsive syncope) that are misdiagnosed as epileptic seizures
An ECG should be obtained in every patient presenting with transient loss of consciousness, regardless of whether seizure is suspected
Mobitz type II AV block with bifascicular block represents advanced infranodal conduction disease and is an indication for permanent pacemaker even when asymptomatic

The post-event recovery pattern is one of the most useful clinical features distinguishing cardiac syncope (immediate recovery) from epileptic seizure (post-ictal confusion typically lasting minutes to hours)
